claim: A release artifact shall be produced by a single atomic build command that fuses population + build + a self-check refusing to emit an artifact whose shipped tool-member names do not match the source-of-truth tool set on disk.
why: Not applicable in the current build model; the original mechanism was specific to a packaging model the framework no longer uses. (Historical note: the uv_build backend packaged only content under src/hotam_spec/; the 26 hotam-* CLI entry points imported their tool scripts from a shipped _tools/ directory populated OUT-OF-BAND just before the build by populate_tools.py; forgetting that manual step shipped a wheel that imported cleanly but had zero working CLI commands, invisible in the repo -- _tools/ was .gitignored. spec/scripts/build_wheel.py fused populate+`uv build --wheel`+verify into one atomic command comparing the SET of _tools/ member names inside the produced .whl against spec/tools/*.py on disk (Etap Y #127, upgraded from a bare member-count comparison to a name-set comparison with an explicit missing/extra diff).) This concern does not arise in the current build model: `go build ./cmd/hotam` compiles all packages natively via the import graph -- there is no out-of-band populate step, no shipped _tools/ directory, and no gap between 'imports cleanly' and 'has working CLI commands'. A release is `go build` producing a single binary; a missing tool would be a compile error, not a silently-broken artifact. The original WHY stands as a design principle (an atomic build with a self-check makes the broken-artifact failure structurally impossible to publish), but the current build model achieves this by construction (the compiler IS the self-check) rather than via a separate verification script. RECLASSIFIED 2026-07-13: enforceability ENFORCEABLE -> INHERENTLY_PROSE (agent consultation, fm) -- the only test available under the current build model would be "go build succeeds", which is already exercised by every other test run in CI and would be a vacuous, non-additive assertion specific to this requirement; the actual guarantee this requirement wants is structurally provided by Go's own compiler (a missing tool reference is a compile error), not by a dedicated enforcer.
